theme: Every static object on this floor is a missed heartbeat — make the room breathe
visual_observations: Desktop 1280×800, Crushing It vibe active. Six worker desks in two rows: Maya/Sofia/Carlos top, Maya/Dave/Sofia bottom. Trophy icons sit inert next to Maya and Sofia — no animation, no count. The coffee machine widget (top-right) is a frozen ☕ emoji above 'COFFEE' — the most visible static prop on the entire floor. The worker status dots ('•••') under each name carry zero information density; you cannot tell who is at 10% vs 90% on their task. The 'SHIP IT' sticky note floats untethered on the left edge — no visual connection to any desk or worker. The window widget (dark rectangle, top-right) reads as a dead black box; its function as a 'window to the outside' is completely lost. The aisle between the two desk rows is unmarked — the floor looks like a parking lot, not a workplace. The idle wandering ring in the lower floor is the only moving element in the bottom half, and it still reads as a loading spinner rather than a human presence.

ideas.title: Coffee Machine Steam Animation
ideas.description: The coffee machine widget (top-right) is a frozen ☕ with a plain COFFEE label — the most conspicuously static prop on the entire floor. Add CSS steam: three pseudo-element wisps (::before, ::after, and a .steam-mid span) each 2px wide, 10px tall, with border-radius: 2px, positioned above the cup. Each warp upward on a keyframe: @keyframes steam-rise { 0% { opacity:0; transform: translateY(0) scaleX(1); } 40% { opacity: 0.55; transform: translateY(-6px) scaleX(1.4); } 100% { opacity:0; transform: translateY(-14px) scaleX(0.7); } }. Stagger delays: 0s, 0.4s, 0.8s, loop infinite 1.8s ease-in-out. Color: rgba(255,255,255,0.35). On vibe='crushing', speed up to 1.1s (add a CSS var --steam-dur that Office.jsx sets via inline style). On vibe='slow-day', slow to 3.5s. On vibe='after-hours', pause animation (animation-play-state: paused — nobody's making coffee). WHY: The coffee machine is spatially prominent and perfectly positioned for ambient life. Three lines of keyframe CSS and a span transforms it from dead clip-art into a room prop you can feel. It also becomes the first vibe-reactive environmental object that isn't a worker.

ideas.title: Desk Progress Bar Strip
ideas.description: The '•••' dots under each working worker's name have been read as a loading spinner or decorative filler in three consecutive user tests — they carry no task-progress meaning. Replace with a thin progress bar: a div.desk__progress-bar (position: absolute, bottom: 6%, left: 10%, width: 80%, height: 3px, border-radius: 2px, background: rgba(255,255,255,0.08)) containing a div.desk__progress-fill (height: 100%, border-radius: 2px, width: driven by worker.taskProgress%, transition: width 0.8s ease). Color the fill by task category: Design → #a78bfa, Bug → #ef4444, Ship → #f97316, QA → #3b82f6, default → var(--accent). When fill reaches 100%, play a 0.4s flash: @keyframes bar-complete { 0%,100% { opacity:1; } 50% { opacity:0.2; background: #ffffff; } }, then the bar resets to 0 on the next tick. Idle workers get opacity:0 on the bar. WHY: This single change converts the desk row from an avatar grid into a live task board. Six progress bars in view simultaneously give a team-wide delivery pulse at a glance — something the floor has never had. It costs ~20 lines of CSS/JSX and eliminates the '•••' ambiguity permanently.

ideas.title: SHIP IT Note Tether Line
ideas.description: The 'SHIP IT' sticky note floats in dead space on the left edge with no visible connection to any worker or desk. A physical sticky note on a real office wall has a string, an arrow, or a position that implies ownership. In Office.jsx, identify the note's assigned worker from its metadata (or default to the nearest desk). Draw an SVG line from the note's right-center [noteEl.right, noteEl.centerY] to the assigned desk's left-center [desk.left, desk.centerY]. Style: stroke rgba(251,191,36,0.35), strokeWidth 1.5, strokeDasharray '5 4'. Animate stroke-dashoffset from 36 to 0 over 1.2s linear infinite — giving a slow 'electricity flowing toward the desk' feel. When the assigned worker is idle, transition stroke to rgba(239,68,68,0.5) over 0.4s. When the note content changes (new task), the line redraws with a 0.25s fade-out/in. The SVG layer is position: absolute, inset:0, pointer-events: none, z-index:1. WHY: The note is the only visible task artifact on the floor and it's spatially orphaned. A tether line makes it a real assignment — 'this sprint goal belongs to that person' — with one animated stroke.

ideas.title: Trophy Unlock Burst Animation
ideas.description: Trophy icons next to Maya and Sofia's desks are fully static — no feedback, no history, no meaning communicated to someone looking at the floor for the first time. Two changes: (1) Add a counter badge — a span.trophy__count (position: absolute, top:-4px, right:-4px, min-width:12px, height:12px, background:var(--accent), border-radius:6px, font-size:0.45rem, color:#fff, display:flex, align-items:center, justify-content:center) showing the integer trophy count. (2) On a new trophy earned (detect via prev vs current trophy count in useEffect), play a burst: four ray divs (absolute, 8×2px, border-radius:1px, background:#fbbf24) rotate-translated outward from the trophy center at 0°/90°/180°/270°, animating from scale(0) opacity(1) at 0% to scale(1.8) opacity(0) at 100% over 0.55s ease-out. The trophy itself scales 1→1.35→1 over 0.4s. All cleanup via setTimeout. WHY: Trophies are the floor's only recognition mechanic and they're silent. A burst that lasts half a second communicates 'something good just happened here' to anyone watching. It is the minimum viable celebration.

ideas.title: Window Widget Sky Gradient
ideas.description: The window widget (dark rectangle, top-right) is a dead black box that reads as a broken image or an off monitor. It is the most evocative prop on the floor — a literal window to the outside world — but it shows nothing. Fix: in the window component, compute hourBucket from new Date().getHours(). Apply a CSS linear-gradient background accordingly: dawn 5-8h → linear-gradient(180deg, #f97316 0%, #fbbf24 60%, #93c5fd 100%); day 8-17h → linear-gradient(180deg, #1d4ed8 0%, #3b82f6 60%, #bfdbfe 100%); dusk 17-20h → linear-gradient(180deg, #7c3aed 0%, #f97316 40%, #fbbf24 100%); night 20-5h → linear-gradient(180deg, #0f172a 0%, #1e1b4b 100%) with three 3×3px white dots (stars, positioned via %). Add a 6px circle .window__sun (background:#fbbf24 day/#f0abfc dusk/#e2e8f0 night for moon, border-radius:50%, position:absolute) at left: calc(10% + (var(--hour-frac) * 80%)), top:30%, where --hour-frac is a 0–1 CSS custom property set by JS from the current hour. WHY: The window is structurally identical to a weather widget and it costs 25 lines. A dawn sky at 6am and a night sky at midnight make the floor time-aware without a single API call.

ideas.title: Office Aisle Marker Between Desk Rows
ideas.description: The two desk rows (top and bottom) share the same visual plane with nothing separating them — the floor looks like an unplanned parking lot rather than a workplace with circulation paths. Add a single horizontal .office-aisle div: position:absolute, left:4%, width:92%, top:52%, height:1px, background: repeating-linear-gradient(90deg, rgba(255,255,255,0.07) 0px, rgba(255,255,255,0.07) 8px, transparent 8px, transparent 16px). Centered on this line, add two tiny .aisle-arrow spans (▶ ◀, font-size:0.45rem, color:rgba(255,255,255,0.12), positioned left:48% and left:51%) suggesting bidirectional foot traffic. On vibe='crushing', aisle opacity bumps to 0.13 (everyone moving fast). On vibe='slow-day', drops to 0.04. On vibe='after-hours', the arrows flip to a single centered ▼ (cleaner-bot direction). WHY: Real open-plan offices are defined by their aisles as much as their desks. One horizontal 1px dashed line with two arrows takes 8 lines of CSS and converts the floor from a floating grid of boxes into a space with implied movement and direction.

ideas.title: Vibe-Change Radial Shockwave
ideas.description: When teamVibe changes (e.g., slow-day → crushing), the floor currently crossfades grid colors over ~0.3s — a settings-panel transition, not an event. The whole team just shifted energy and the room whispers about it. Instead, fire a radial shockwave from Bloberto's desk center on every vibe change. Implementation: a .vibe-shockwave div (position:absolute, border-radius:50%, pointer-events:none, z-index:5) mounted via React state on vibeKey change. Initial state: width:0, height:0, centered at Bloberto desk coords, border: 2px solid [new vibe's accent color], opacity: 0.7. Animate via CSS @keyframes shockwave { to { width: 140%; height: 140%; opacity: 0; border-width: 0.5px; } } over 0.9s cubic-bezier(0.2, 0.8, 0.4, 1). Component self-removes via useEffect cleanup at 900ms. That is the entire implementation. WHY: A vibe change is the most significant state event in Bloberto's Office — it means the whole team's energy just shifted. A shockwave that blooms from the boss's desk and fades over a second transforms a CSS variable swap into a moment of communal narrative. It costs 15 lines and it will be the single most-noticed animation on the floor.
